title: One thing I love about this solo, zombie/survival RPG (The Walking Dead Universe)
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=a8g5MvFPwxo

Daniel (@dungeondive) takes a look at **The Walking Dead Universe RPG** from Free League — a comp copy from the publisher, with full coverage promised for a later video. He approaches the game from an unusual angle: he is *not* a Walking Dead TV-show fan; he is here because the *Here's Negan* board game has earned his admiration, and because he wants a solo-friendly RPG outside his usual fantasy wheelhouse.

The thing he loves: the **random locations table**. His fantasy shelf is dense with random-encounter tools (decks, zines, oracles); his *mundane-modern* shelf is sparse, and Walking Dead Universe fills the gap. The table is a d66 roll into a 36-entry primary list, with a d6 sub-table for each — meaning 216 possible micro-scenarios. He walks three: a suburban family home where rats have overrun the place and the dead are chasing the rats (the rats can be used as a distraction); a prison with torn-down inner walls and shambling former prisoners (potentially convertible to a Haven); a karate club where someone has *cut the limbs off* a roomful of walkers, leaving the dismembered bodies crawling the dojo floor toward whatever weapons remain on the walls.

Matt Kay's solo rules also earn praise — well-structured, well-paced, and unusually approachable for board-gamers crossing into solo RPG for the first time.

----

Daniel calls out a shelf imbalance: lots of tools for randomising fantasy, very few for randomising modern/mundane settings. Is that imbalance the genre's fault, or just a side-effect of the kinds of games solo players gravitate to?
